options.explanation: Wrong. Idle most of the day.
Wrong. Premium fixed cost.

questions.stem: Which feature protects against accidental deletion of S3 objects?

options.explanation: Correct. Versioning retains prior versions, guarding against deletes/overwrites.
Wrong. That speeds uploads.
Wrong. Encryption protects confidentiality, not accidental deletion.
Wrong. CDN caching does not protect against deletes.

questions.stem: Which service provides a managed certificate for TLS on a load balancer at no extra cost?

options.explanation: Correct. ACM issues and renews TLS certificates for ELB/CloudFront.
Wrong. KMS manages encryption keys, not TLS certs for ELB.
Wrong. ACM is the modern managed approach.
Wrong. That stores secrets, not public TLS certs for ELB.

questions.stem: Which service is best for a managed GraphQL API backend?

options.explanation: Correct. AppSync is a managed GraphQL service.
Wrong. API Gateway is primarily REST/HTTP/WebSocket, not native GraphQL managed service.
Wrong. SQS is a queue.
Wrong. SNS is pub/sub.

questions.stem: Which approach reduces inter-AZ data transfer charges for chatty services?

options.text: Co-locate tightly-coupled services within the same AZ where resilience allows
options.explanation: Correct. Keeping chatty traffic in one AZ avoids cross-AZ charges.

options.text: Spread every component across all AZs regardless of traffic
options.explanation: Wrong. That can increase cross-AZ transfer cost.

options.text: Use a NAT gateway between them
options.explanation: Wrong. NAT adds cost and is for egress.
Wrong. Less secure and not cheaper.

questions.stem: Which database option provides automatic failover within seconds for Aurora?

options.text: Aurora replicas in multiple AZs with automatic failover
options.explanation: Correct. Aurora promotes a replica automatically on primary failure.
Wrong. One instance has no failover target.
Wrong. That is slow, not seconds.
Wrong. Backups are not automatic failover.

questions.stem: Which mechanism lets an application retrieve database credentials securely at runtime with rotation?

options.text: Fetch from AWS Secrets Manager via the instance role
options.explanation: Correct. Secrets Manager plus an IAM role delivers rotated credentials securely.
Wrong. Baked credentials are hard to rotate and leak-prone.

options.text: Store them in a public S3 object
options.explanation: Wrong. Public storage of secrets is a severe risk.
Wrong. User data is visible and not rotated.

questions.stem: Which option improves database read scalability for a globally distributed read audience with low latency?

options.text: Aurora Global Database with cross-Region read replicas
options.explanation: Correct. Global Database serves low-latency reads across Regions.
Wrong. Distant readers face high latency.
Wrong. Local is a dev tool, not a global service.
Wrong. Storage size does not address global read latency.

questions.stem: Which is a quick cost optimization for over-provisioned EBS volumes?

options.text: Right-size or change to a cheaper volume type based on actual IOPS/throughput
options.explanation: Correct. Matching volume type/size to real usage cuts cost.

options.text: Always use io2 for every volume
options.explanation: Wrong. io2 is premium and wasteful for low-IOPS needs.
Wrong. That adds cost.
Wrong. Unnecessary and not a cost saver.

questions.stem: Which service provides managed, automated patching and configuration of EC2 fleets?

options.explanation: Correct. Systems Manager automates patching, config, and operations.
Wrong. CloudFormation provisions infra, not ongoing patch automation.
Wrong. Inspector finds vulnerabilities; it does not patch.
Wrong. Config tracks configuration state.

questions.stem: Which network control is stateless and evaluated at the subnet boundary?

options.explanation: Correct. NACLs are stateless subnet-level filters.
Wrong. Security groups are stateful and instance-level.
Wrong. IAM governs API permissions.
Wrong. Route tables direct traffic, they do not filter by allow/deny rules.

questions.stem: Which service provides a fully managed, in-VPC, horizontally scalable graph database?

options.explanation: Correct. Neptune is a managed graph database.
Wrong. RDS is relational.
Wrong. DynamoDB is key-value/document, not a graph engine.
Wrong. Redshift is a data warehouse.

questions.stem: Which service captures and stores VPC network traffic metadata for security analysis?

options.explanation: Correct. Flow Logs record IP traffic metadata for a VPC, subnet, or ENI.
Wrong. Those record CDN requests, not VPC traffic.
Wrong. Those record bucket access, not network flows.
Wrong. Those record DNS queries, not IP flow data.

questions.stem: Which approach reduces cost for sending large volumes of data out to the internet repeatedly?

options.text: Cache and serve through CloudFront to cut origin egress
options.explanation: Correct. Edge caching lowers repeated data-transfer-out charges.

options.text: Send everything directly from EC2 each time
options.explanation: Wrong. Repeated origin egress is costlier.
Wrong. EIPs do not reduce transfer cost.
Wrong. Instance size does not lower egress charges.

questions.stem: Which feature lets you log and continuously evaluate resource configurations against compliance rules?

options.explanation: Correct. AWS Config records configuration and evaluates compliance rules.
Wrong. CloudTrail records API calls, not continuous config compliance.
Wrong. Alarms watch metrics, not resource compliance.
Wrong. Shield is DDoS protection.

questions.stem: A retail site runs on EC2 behind an ALB in one AZ. Last month an AZ outage took the site down for 4 hours. What change prevents a repeat with minimal re-architecture?

options.text: Spread the Auto Scaling group across at least two AZs behind the same ALB
options.explanation: Correct. Multi-AZ ASG keeps serving traffic when one AZ fails. `aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group --auto-scaling-group-name web --vpc-zone-identifier subnet-a,subnet-b --min-size 2 ...`.

options.text: Move to a larger instance type in the same AZ
options.explanation: Wrong. Vertical scaling keeps the single-AZ failure point.

options.text: Add CloudFront in front of the ALB
options.explanation: Wrong. A CDN caches content but cannot serve dynamic requests when the only origin AZ is down.
Wrong. Backups shorten rebuild time but do not prevent the outage.

questions.stem: A healthcare company must ensure objects in an S3 bucket are never publicly readable, even if a developer misconfigures an object ACL. What enforces this account-wide?

options.text: Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level
options.explanation: Correct. Block Public Access overrides ACLs and policies that would grant public access. Enforce with `aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket NAME --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true`.

options.text: Rely on developers to set private ACLs
options.explanation: Wrong. Human process is exactly what failed in the scenario.
Wrong. Encryption protects data at rest, not against public access grants.
Wrong. Versioning preserves history; it does not block public reads.

questions.stem: A genomics lab runs a 6-hour nightly analysis job that can checkpoint and resume. Compute cost is the top complaint. Which change cuts cost the most?

options.text: Run the job on Spot Instances with checkpointing to S3
options.explanation: Correct. Interruptible, resumable batch work is the ideal Spot use case.

options.text: Buy a 3-year Reserved Instance for the job server
options.explanation: Wrong. Paying 24/7 commitment for a 6-hour nightly job wastes most of the reservation.

options.text: Move the job to a larger On-Demand instance
options.explanation: Wrong. Bigger On-Demand finishes faster but costs more per hour.

options.text: Run it on a Dedicated Host
options.explanation: Wrong. Dedicated Hosts are premium-priced for licensing needs.

questions.stem: A news API serves the same trending-articles query thousands of times a minute, and the RDS database is at 90% CPU. Which fix gives the fastest relief without schema changes?

options.text: Add ElastiCache and cache the trending-articles result with a short TTL
options.explanation: Correct. Caching repeated identical reads offloads the database immediately. Front the DB with ElastiCache; e.g. `aws elasticache create-cache-cluster --cache-cluster-id app --engine redis --num-cache-nodes 1`.

options.text: Shard the database across three instances
options.explanation: Wrong. Sharding is a major re-architecture for what is a cache problem.
Wrong. That accelerates S3 uploads, unrelated to database reads.
Wrong. Protocol changes do not reduce database query load.

questions.stem: A payments platform requires RPO under 1 second and RTO under 1 minute for its Aurora database if a Region fails. Which design meets this?

options.text: Aurora Global Database with a secondary Region promoted on failure
options.explanation: Correct. Global Database replicates with sub-second lag and promotes in under a minute. `aws rds create-global-cluster --global-cluster-identifier app-global --source-db-cluster-identifier <arn>`.

options.text: Nightly snapshots copied to another Region
options.explanation: Wrong. Snapshot-based recovery means hours of data loss and restore time.
Wrong. Multi-AZ does not survive a full Region outage.

options.text: Export to S3 with cross-Region replication
options.explanation: Wrong. Export-based recovery cannot meet a 1-second RPO.

questions.stem: An auditor asks for proof of every console and API action taken in the production account over the past year. The team enabled nothing beyond defaults. What should have been configured?

options.text: A CloudTrail trail delivering to S3 with log file validation
options.explanation: Correct. A configured trail retains and protects a full API audit history; default event history only covers 90 days. Create with `aws cloudtrail create-trail --name org-trail --s3-bucket-name logs-bkt` then `aws cloudtrail start-logging --name org-trail`.
Wrong. That collects metrics, not API audit records.
Wrong. Flow logs capture network metadata, not console/API actions.
Wrong. Config tracks resource state changes, not every API call.

questions.stem: A mobile game's leaderboard needs single-digit-millisecond reads and writes for millions of concurrent players worldwide, with a simple key-based access pattern. Which database fits?

options.explanation: Correct. Key-value access at extreme scale with multi-Region replication is DynamoDB's core use case.
Wrong. Relational replicas add lag and do not scale writes globally.
Wrong. Redshift is for analytics, not millisecond OLTP.
Wrong. Object storage cannot serve millisecond transactional reads/writes per player.

questions.stem: A startup's AWS bill doubled and nobody knows why. Which first step identifies the cause fastest?

options.text: Open Cost Explorer and group costs by service and usage type
options.explanation: Correct. Cost Explorer breaks down spend trends to pinpoint the increase.
Wrong. Acting before diagnosing risks deleting the wrong things.
Wrong. CloudTrail audits API calls, not cost attribution.
Wrong. A blanket change without diagnosis may break production.

questions.stem: An order-processing system loses orders when the downstream fulfillment service crashes. Orders must never be lost and must process in the order received per customer. What fits?

options.text: An SQS FIFO queue between the services with message group IDs per customer
options.explanation: Correct. FIFO queues guarantee ordering per group and retain messages until processed.

options.text: Direct synchronous API calls with retries
options.explanation: Wrong. If the consumer is down long enough, requests are still dropped.
Wrong. SNS without a queue does not retain messages for an offline consumer.
Wrong. Standard queues do not guarantee per-customer ordering.

questions.stem: Developers SSH into production EC2 instances using a shared key pair, which failed a security review. What removes SSH keys entirely while keeping shell access auditable?

options.text: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager with IAM-controlled access
options.explanation: Correct. Session Manager gives keyless, logged shell sessions governed by IAM.
Wrong. A shared credential remains unauditable per user.

options.text: Move instances to public subnets with security groups
options.explanation: Wrong. That increases exposure and keeps keys.

options.text: Use a bastion host with the same shared key
options.explanation: Wrong. A bastion narrows the entry point but the shared-key problem remains.

questions.stem: Users in Australia complain a US-hosted web app's static assets load slowly. The dynamic API is acceptable. What is the lowest-effort fix?

options.explanation: Correct. Edge caching brings static content close to Australian users with no app changes.

options.text: Deploy the full stack to ap-southeast-2
options.explanation: Wrong. A second Region deployment is far more effort than the problem requires.
Wrong. Server capacity does not fix trans-Pacific latency for static files.

options.text: Enable RDS read replicas in Sydney
options.explanation: Wrong. The complaint is static assets, not database reads.

questions.stem: A SaaS company runs 40 production EC2 instances at steady 24/7 utilization and expects this for years. They also run unpredictable dev workloads. How should they buy compute?

options.text: Compute Savings Plan sized to the steady baseline; On-Demand or Spot for dev
options.explanation: Correct. Commit to the predictable floor, stay flexible for variable load.

options.text: Savings Plan covering peak including dev
options.explanation: Wrong. Committing to peak pays for capacity that often sits idle.
Wrong. The steady 24/7 baseline is exactly what commitments discount.
Wrong. Production cannot tolerate Spot interruption.

questions.stem: A WordPress site on a single EC2 instance stores uploads on the instance disk. Adding a second instance behind an ALB broke image uploads. What fixes shared media storage?

options.text: Move uploads to Amazon EFS mounted on both instances (or offload to S3)
options.explanation: Correct. Shared file or object storage lets all instances see the same media.

options.text: Use EBS Multi-Attach for the web volume
options.explanation: Wrong. Multi-Attach requires cluster-aware filesystems and is not for general web content.
Wrong. Stickiness routes users but each instance still has different files.
Wrong. Instance store is ephemeral and still per-instance.

questions.stem: A company must encrypt sensitive S3 data with keys it can rotate and revoke, and must log every use of the key. Which TWO services accomplish this?

options.explanation: Correct. KMS-managed encryption ties object access to a controllable key. `aws kms create-key` then reference the key id when enabling encryption.

options.text: AWS KMS with a customer managed key and CloudTrail logging
options.explanation: Correct. Customer managed keys support rotation, revocation, and audited usage. `aws kms create-key` then reference the key id when enabling encryption.
Wrong. S3-managed keys cannot be revoked or usage-audited by the customer.
Wrong. Ad hoc passwords are unmanaged and unauditable.

questions.stem: An IoT fleet sends 50,000 sensor readings per second that must be processed in real time and replayed if a consumer fails. Which ingestion service fits?

options.explanation: Correct. Kinesis handles high-throughput ordered streams with replay from the retention window.
Wrong. SQS deletes consumed messages and is not designed for replayable high-volume streams.
Wrong. Millions of small PUTs per minute is costly and not real-time processing.
Wrong. A relational database is not a streaming ingestion layer at this rate.

questions.stem: A media archive stores raw footage that is accessed within minutes of upload for a week, rarely for a year after, and almost never beyond that but must be retained. Which lifecycle is most cost-effective?

options.text: S3 Standard, transition to Standard-IA after 30 days, then Glacier Deep Archive after a year
options.explanation: Correct. The lifecycle matches each access phase to the cheapest suitable class. e.g. `aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration --bucket NAME --lifecycle-configuration file://lc.json`.
Wrong. Paying hot-storage rates for cold data wastes money.

options.text: Move to Deep Archive after one week
options.explanation: Wrong. The footage is still rarely accessed during the year; IA serves that at instant retrieval.
Wrong. New footage is hot for a week, and One Zone risks loss of irreplaceable raw media.

questions.stem: A trading app must keep serving reads even if its primary database fails, with failover under 30 seconds and zero manual steps. It uses Aurora MySQL. What configuration delivers this?

options.text: Aurora replicas in other AZs with automatic failover priority set
options.explanation: Correct. Aurora promotes a replica automatically, typically within 30 seconds.

options.text: A single writer with daily snapshots
options.explanation: Wrong. Snapshot restores take far longer and are manual.
Wrong. Cross-account manual promotion violates the zero-manual-step requirement.

options.text: RDS MySQL single-AZ with larger instance
options.explanation: Wrong. No standby means downtime on failure.

questions.stem: A third-party analytics vendor needs read access to one S3 prefix in your account for 6 months. What is the most secure arrangement?

options.text: A cross-account IAM role scoped to that prefix that the vendor assumes
options.explanation: Correct. Scoped role assumption gives temporary, auditable, revocable access.

options.text: Create an IAM user and email them the access keys
options.explanation: Wrong. Long-lived shared keys are leak-prone and hard to audit.

options.text: Make the prefix public for the engagement
options.explanation: Wrong. Public exposure grants access to everyone, not one vendor.
Wrong. Sharing personal credentials is never acceptable.

questions.stem: A video platform transcodes uploads with jobs lasting 1-15 minutes, spiking from zero to thousands per hour unpredictably. Which compute minimizes both queueing delay and idle cost?

options.text: AWS Batch (or ECS) on Fargate Spot driven by an SQS queue
options.explanation: Correct. Serverless containers scale with the queue and cost nothing when idle; Spot cuts cost for retryable jobs.

options.text: A fixed fleet of 50 EC2 instances
options.explanation: Wrong. Fixed fleets idle between spikes and queue during them.
Wrong. 15-minute transcodes hit Lambda's execution ceiling and memory limits for video.
Wrong. A single instance is a bottleneck and a single point of failure.

questions.stem: After a project ended, a team discovered they paid three months for idle resources: stopped-but-not-deleted EBS volumes, unattached Elastic IPs, and an unused NAT gateway. What prevents this class of waste going forward?

options.text: AWS Budgets alerts plus regular Trusted Advisor / Cost Explorer idle-resource reviews
options.explanation: Correct. Alerts catch anomalies and idle-resource checks surface orphaned resources.

options.text: Switch the account to a Savings Plan
options.explanation: Wrong. Commitments discount usage; they do not find orphaned resources.
Wrong. API auditing does not flag idle spend.
Wrong. Consolidation is unrelated to forgotten orphaned resources.

questions.stem: A ticketing site expects a 50x traffic spike when sales open at 9 AM Friday. The web tier autoscales, but scaling takes minutes and the first wave of users gets errors. What fixes the cold start?

options.text: Scheduled scaling to pre-warm capacity before 9 AM
options.explanation: Correct. Scheduled actions raise minimum capacity ahead of a known spike.
Wrong. A higher threshold makes scaling react later, not earlier.
Wrong. Smaller instances scale out in more units but still take minutes to launch.
Wrong. The bottleneck described is web-tier launch latency, not the database.

questions.stem: A company wants every new AWS account in its organization to be blocked from disabling CloudTrail or leaving the approved Regions, regardless of the account admins' IAM permissions. What enforces this?

options.text: Service Control Policies attached in AWS Organizations
options.explanation: Correct. SCPs are guardrails that cap permissions even for account administrators.
Wrong. Account admins can edit their own IAM policies.
Wrong. Documents do not technically prevent actions.
Wrong. Alarms detect after the fact; they do not block.

questions.stem: A reporting team runs ad-hoc SQL on 200 GB of CSV logs already in S3, a few times a week. Standing infrastructure feels wasteful. What is the right query approach?

options.text: Amazon Athena directly against the S3 data
options.explanation: Correct. Serverless SQL on S3 with per-query pricing suits occasional ad-hoc analysis.

options.text: Load everything into a permanent Redshift cluster
options.explanation: Wrong. An always-on warehouse for occasional queries wastes money.

options.text: Spin up RDS and import the CSVs weekly
options.explanation: Wrong. Repeated imports add toil for no benefit.
Wrong. 200 GB local processing is slow and unscalable.

questions.stem: An application makes heavy S3 GET requests from EC2 in the same Region through a NAT gateway, and NAT data-processing charges dominate the bill. What removes those charges?

options.text: Add an S3 gateway VPC endpoint and route S3 traffic through it
options.explanation: Correct. Gateway endpoints carry S3 traffic privately at no data-processing charge.
Wrong. More NAT gateways add cost; traffic is still NAT-processed.
Wrong. That speeds long-distance uploads and adds cost.
Wrong. Compression shrinks but does not eliminate NAT processing charges.

questions.stem: A logistics firm's on-prem data center hosts the system of record. They need AWS workloads to keep functioning during brief internet outages and want private, consistent network performance to AWS. What connectivity fits?

options.text: AWS Direct Connect with a Site-to-Site VPN as backup
options.explanation: Correct. Direct Connect gives consistent private connectivity; VPN covers circuit failure.
Wrong. Internet-based VPN has variable performance and shares the outage risk described.
Wrong. That accepts the exact outages they want to mitigate.

options.text: Two VPNs over the same ISP
options.explanation: Wrong. Same-provider redundancy fails together.

questions.stem: A regulated firm must prove an S3 object written for compliance has not been modified or deleted for 7 years, even by administrators. Which configuration satisfies this?

options.text: S3 Object Lock in compliance mode with a 7-year retention period
options.explanation: Correct. Compliance mode prevents modification or deletion by anyone, including root, until retention expires.
Wrong. Admins can change policies and delete versions.
Wrong. Governance mode can be bypassed by users with special permission.
Wrong. Storage class affects cost/retrieval, not immutability.

questions.stem: A B2B API has bursty traffic and a hard requirement: no request management servers to patch. It must authenticate callers, throttle them, and invoke business logic. Which stack fits?

options.text: API Gateway with Lambda authorizers and Lambda backends
options.explanation: Correct. Fully managed API fronting serverless compute meets auth, throttling, and zero-patching.
Wrong. EC2 means servers to patch.
Wrong. A CDN does not provide request auth/throttling/business logic.
Wrong. Queue polling is not a synchronous authenticated API, and EC2 needs patching.

questions.stem: A dev team keeps RDS, EC2, and ElastiCache running nights and weekends for an app only used 9-5 weekdays. Management wants savings without slowing developers. What is the best move?

options.text: Automate stop/start on a weekday schedule (Instance Scheduler)
options.explanation: Correct. Off-hours shutdown cuts roughly two-thirds of compute hours invisibly to working hours.

options.text: Reserved Instances for the dev stack
options.explanation: Wrong. RIs charge for the idle nights and weekends too.

options.text: Delete the environment nightly and rebuild
options.explanation: Wrong. Rebuild friction slows developers; stop/start preserves state.
Wrong. Sharing production is a reliability and safety regression.

questions.stem: A company's static marketing site must stay up through Region-scale incidents and viral traffic spikes, with near-zero ops effort. What architecture fits?

options.text: S3 static hosting with CloudFront in front
options.explanation: Correct. Edge-cached object storage absorbs spikes and survives origin-Region issues from cache.

options.text: Auto Scaled EC2 web servers in one Region
options.explanation: Wrong. More ops effort and still Region-bound.

options.text: A single large EC2 instance with high bandwidth
options.explanation: Wrong. A single instance is a single point of failure.
Wrong. Re-rendering static pages per request adds cost and complexity for no benefit.

questions.stem: After a contractor's laptop was stolen, the security team wants console access to require something beyond passwords for all human users. What is the direct control?

options.text: Enforce MFA for all IAM users and IAM Identity Center sign-ins
options.explanation: Correct. MFA adds a second factor exactly addressing stolen-credential risk.
Wrong. A stolen password of any length is still one factor.
Wrong. Helpful, but breaks remote work and is not a second factor.
Wrong. Keys address programmatic access, not console password risk.

questions.stem: A real-time bidding service requires sub-millisecond data lookups for session state shared across instances. DynamoDB at 5 ms is too slow. What achieves the latency target?

options.text: ElastiCache for Redis (or DAX in front of DynamoDB)
options.explanation: Correct. In-memory stores serve microsecond-to-sub-millisecond reads for shared state.
Wrong. Disk-backed relational reads cannot reach sub-millisecond consistently.
Wrong. Object storage latency is tens of milliseconds.
Wrong. Network file reads do not meet sub-millisecond targets.

questions.stem: An internal app's ALB health checks pass, but users report intermittent 5xx errors traced to one bad instance that passes shallow checks. How should health checking improve?

options.text: Point the health check at an application endpoint that exercises real dependencies
options.explanation: Correct. Deep health checks catch instances that are up but not functioning.
Wrong. Slower checks detect failures later.
Wrong. That is shallower than the current check.

options.text: Remove health checks to avoid flapping
options.explanation: Wrong. No checks means bad instances stay in service forever.

questions.stem: A company's data scientists each spin up large GPU notebooks and forget them overnight. The platform team must cap this waste without banning GPUs. What combination works?

options.text: Idle-shutdown lifecycle configs plus budget alerts per team
options.explanation: Correct. Auto-stop addresses forgetting; alerts create accountability.

options.text: Block GPU instance types via SCP
options.explanation: Wrong. That bans the legitimate work, not the waste.
Wrong. Commitments make idle overnight hours cheaper, not eliminated.
Wrong. The scenario shows process alone fails.

questions.stem: A web app behind ALB is getting credential-stuffing login attempts and SQL-injection probes. Which managed service blocks these patterns at the edge of the application?

options.text: AWS WAF with managed rule groups on the ALB
options.explanation: Correct. WAF inspects HTTP requests and blocks injection and credential-stuffing patterns. `aws wafv2 create-web-acl --name app --scope REGIONAL ...` and associate it with the ALB.
Wrong. SGs filter by port/IP, not request content.
Wrong. Shield mitigates DDoS, not application-layer injection.
Wrong. Subnet ACLs cannot inspect HTTP payloads.

questions.stem: A global SaaS adds customers in Europe who complain about API latency to the us-east-1 backend. Compliance allows EU processing. What architecture change helps most?

options.text: Deploy a second full stack in an EU Region with latency-based Route 53 routing
options.explanation: Correct. Serving EU users from an EU Region removes transatlantic round trips.
Wrong. Capacity does not reduce geographic latency.

options.text: CloudFront in front of the API only
options.explanation: Wrong. Dynamic uncacheable API calls still traverse to us-east-1 (TLS termination helps marginally).
Wrong. VPNs do not shorten the physical path meaningfully.

questions.stem: An architecture review of a single-AZ application found these risks. Which TWO changes most directly improve availability?

options.text: Move the database to RDS Multi-AZ
options.explanation: Correct. A synchronous standby removes the database single point of failure.

options.text: Run the app tier in an Auto Scaling group across multiple AZs
options.explanation: Correct. Multi-AZ compute survives an AZ loss.
Wrong. Better metrics observe failures; they do not prevent downtime.
Wrong. Tags aid governance, not availability.

questions.stem: A firm stores 500 TB of compliance archives in S3 Standard 'to be safe.' Files are retrieved perhaps twice a year, and a 12-hour retrieval delay is acceptable. Annual storage cost must drop dramatically. What change is right?

options.text: Lifecycle the archives to S3 Glacier Deep Archive
options.explanation: Correct. Deep Archive cuts storage cost by roughly 95% and meets the 12-hour retrieval tolerance.
Wrong. It helps unknown patterns, but known-cold data should go straight to Deep Archive for maximum savings.
Wrong. Modest savings and reduced resilience for irreplaceable compliance data.
Wrong. Compression helps but Standard rates still dominate vs. archive tiers.

questions.stem: A Lambda function needs a database password. The current code has it hardcoded, flagged in review. The fix must support automatic rotation. What is the right pattern?

options.text: Store the secret in Secrets Manager and fetch it via the execution role
options.explanation: Correct. Secrets Manager provides IAM-gated retrieval with built-in rotation.

options.text: Move it to a Lambda environment variable in plaintext
options.explanation: Wrong. Better than code, but still static, visible in config, and unrotated.

options.text: Put it in an S3 object
options.explanation: Wrong. S3 is not a secrets manager and has no rotation.

options.text: Encode it in base64 in code
options.explanation: Wrong. Encoding is not encryption.

questions.stem: A machine-learning team's training jobs read millions of small files from S3 and are I/O bound. They need a high-throughput POSIX filesystem view of the S3 data for the GPU cluster. What service provides this?

options.text: Amazon FSx for Lustre linked to the S3 bucket
options.explanation: Correct. FSx for Lustre presents S3 data as a high-performance parallel filesystem.
Wrong. EFS is general-purpose NFS, not optimized for ML-scale parallel throughput from S3.

options.text: EBS volumes copied from S3 nightly
options.explanation: Wrong. Manual copies add staleness and ops burden.
Wrong. FUSE mounts are slow for metadata-heavy small-file training.

questions.stem: A nightly ETL must run steps in order—extract, validate, transform, load—with retries per step and an alert if any step fails twice. The team wants no orchestration servers. What service coordinates this?

options.explanation: Correct. State machines give ordered steps, per-step retries, and failure branching, serverlessly.
Wrong. That is an orchestration server with hand-rolled retry logic.
Wrong. Pub/sub fan-out has no state, ordering control, or retry policy per step.
Wrong. A monolith loses per-step retries and hits timeout limits.

questions.stem: A team runs Kubernetes on EKS with 30 always-on m5.2xlarge nodes averaging 25% utilization. What change saves the most while keeping the platform?

options.text: Right-size with smaller nodes plus the cluster autoscaler (or Karpenter), and add Spot for tolerant workloads
options.explanation: Correct. Matching capacity to the 25% utilization and scaling dynamically removes most idle cost.
Wrong. Rewriting a Kubernetes platform is not a sizing fix.

options.text: Buy RIs for all 30 nodes as-is
options.explanation: Wrong. Discounting 75% idle capacity locks in the waste.
Wrong. Larger nodes at 25% use waste more.

questions.stem: An engineer accidentally committed AWS access keys to a public GitHub repo an hour ago. What is the correct first action?

options.text: Deactivate/delete the exposed keys immediately, then audit CloudTrail for misuse
options.explanation: Correct. Kill the credential first; investigation follows.
Wrong. Scrapers harvest keys within minutes; the keys themselves must die.
Wrong. Communication without revocation leaves the door open.
Wrong. Exposed keys are exploited in minutes, not weeks.

questions.stem: An analytics dashboard queries a 2 TB events table in RDS PostgreSQL, slowing the production OLTP workload. Reporting can tolerate minutes of staleness. What separates the workloads with least effort?

options.text: Add an RDS read replica and point the dashboard at it
options.explanation: Correct. Replicas offload read-heavy reporting with acceptable replication lag.
Wrong. A full warehouse migration is heavy for the stated need.
Wrong. That limits the business instead of fixing the architecture.
Wrong. Co-located workloads still contend; cost rises.

questions.stem: An application processes user uploads via a fleet of workers. During deploys, in-flight jobs are killed and lost. Jobs are idempotent and take under 5 minutes. What makes deploys lossless?

options.text: Put jobs in SQS; workers delete messages only after success, and visibility timeout covers processing
options.explanation: Correct. Unacknowledged messages reappear for another worker, so killed jobs are retried.
Wrong. That trades data loss for availability loss.

options.text: Write jobs to local disk first
options.explanation: Wrong. Local disk dies with the instance.
Wrong. Notice helps but offers no retry guarantee like a queue does.

questions.stem: A multi-account organization wants one place where security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, and Macie across all accounts are aggregated, normalized, and tracked. Which service is designed for this?

options.text: AWS Security Hub with a delegated administrator account
options.explanation: Correct. Security Hub aggregates and normalizes findings across services and accounts.
Wrong. Hand-built dashboards do not normalize or centralize findings.

options.text: An S3 bucket of exported reports
options.explanation: Wrong. Static exports lack tracking and workflow.
Wrong. Advisor gives best-practice checks, not multi-account security finding aggregation.

questions.stem: A stock-quote API must return cached prices within 10 ms to clients worldwide, with TCP-level performance and static IPs that enterprise firewalls can allowlist. CloudFront alone was rejected for the static-IP requirement. What fits?

options.text: AWS Global Accelerator in front of Regional endpoints
options.explanation: Correct. Anycast static IPs with AWS-backbone routing meets both latency and allowlisting needs.
Wrong. DNS provides no static IPs and no backbone acceleration.
Wrong. Static, yes—but no global low latency.
Wrong. CloudFront IPs rotate, failing the allowlist requirement.

questions.stem: Finance wants showback: monthly AWS cost broken down by product team across shared accounts. Engineering will not split accounts this quarter. What enables team-level cost reporting now?

options.text: Enforce cost-allocation tags (team=...) and report via Cost Explorer / CUR by tag
options.explanation: Correct. Activated cost-allocation tags slice shared-account spend by team.
Wrong. Tags deliver showback without restructuring.
Wrong. Guesswork undermines the purpose of showback.

options.text: Give finance read access to every console
options.explanation: Wrong. Console access is not a cost-attribution mechanism.

questions.stem: A SaaS provider promises tenants their data survives the loss of any single AZ and any single Region's S3 outage. Tenant files live in S3. Which storage design honors the promise?

options.text: S3 (already multi-AZ) plus Cross-Region Replication to a second Region
options.explanation: Correct. S3 covers AZ loss natively; CRR covers a Regional S3 outage. `aws s3api put-bucket-replication --bucket SRC --replication-configuration file://rep.json`.
Wrong. One Zone explicitly does not survive AZ loss.
Wrong. Standard survives AZ loss but not a Regional outage.

options.text: EBS snapshots of an S3 sync server
options.explanation: Wrong. Convoluted, and snapshots live in the same Region.

questions.stem: Auditors require that all data moving between the company VPC and S3 never traverses the public internet. What satisfies the requirement?

options.text: A gateway VPC endpoint for S3 with routes and endpoint policies
options.explanation: Correct. Gateway endpoints keep S3 traffic on the AWS private network.
Wrong. Encrypted, but still over public address space—auditors asked for private path.
Wrong. NAT egress goes to the public internet.
Wrong. Acceleration uses public edge endpoints.

questions.stem: A document search feature must support full-text queries with relevance ranking, fuzzy matching, and aggregations over 50 million documents. The current LIKE-based RDS queries time out. What is the right tool?

options.explanation: Correct. Full-text relevance, fuzziness, and aggregations are exactly what a search engine provides.
Wrong. B-tree indexes cannot deliver relevance-ranked fuzzy full-text search at scale.
Wrong. Key-prefix matching is not full-text search.
Wrong. Batch SQL scans are not low-latency ranked search.

questions.stem: A subscription service runs its scheduler as a cron job on one EC2 instance. When that instance was terminated by mistake, renewals stopped for a day. What removes this single point of failure with least change?

options.text: Move the schedule to EventBridge Scheduler invoking Lambda (or ECS task)
options.explanation: Correct. Managed scheduling has no instance to lose.

options.text: Run the same cron on two instances
options.explanation: Wrong. Duplicate crons double-fire jobs; coordination becomes a new problem.

options.text: Enable termination protection on the instance
options.explanation: Wrong. Protection helps against one mistake but the instance can still fail.
Wrong. Restores are slow and manual; the SPOF remains.

questions.stem: An EC2-hosted app calls DynamoDB, S3, and SQS. Security found long-lived access keys in its config file. What is the correct remediation?

options.text: Delete the keys and attach an IAM role to the instance with least-privilege policies
options.explanation: Correct. Instance roles supply auto-rotated temporary credentials with no stored secrets.
Wrong. The app must decrypt it, and long-lived keys persist.
Wrong. Still static long-lived credentials, just relocated.

options.text: Rotate the keys weekly via script
options.explanation: Wrong. Manual rotation of static keys is the anti-pattern roles eliminate.

questions.stem: A B2B platform's tenants each get an RDS instance, most sitting under 5% utilization. The bill is dominated by these idle databases. Tenants need isolation at the schema level only. What re-architecture saves most?

options.text: Consolidate tenants onto shared instances (or Aurora Serverless v2) with per-tenant schemas
options.explanation: Correct. Pooling near-idle databases removes per-tenant fixed cost; serverless scales with actual load.
Wrong. Discounting hundreds of idle instances locks in the waste.
Wrong. Marginal; the per-instance floor cost remains.

options.text: Move every tenant to its own account
options.explanation: Wrong. More isolation than required, and more overhead.

questions.stem: A photo-sharing app generates five thumbnail sizes synchronously during upload, making uploads take 8 seconds. Users abandon. What architecture makes uploads fast?

options.text: Accept the original to S3, trigger thumbnailing asynchronously via S3 events to Lambda
options.explanation: Correct. Decoupling moves slow work off the request path; uploads return immediately.
Wrong. Throughput rises but each upload still does 8 seconds of work inline.
Wrong. Possible, but shifts latency to viewers and complicates caching; async pre-generation is the standard fix.
Wrong. Constraining users is not an architecture.

questions.stem: A hospital system needs its patient-portal database in two Regions: writable in the primary, promoted in under a minute if the primary Region fails, with replication lag typically under a second. Which AWS database meets all three?

options.explanation: Correct. Storage-level replication with sub-second typical lag and fast cross-Region promotion.
Wrong. Logical replication lags more and promotion is slower/manual.
Wrong. Multi-Region, yes—but the scenario specifies a relational patient-portal database.
Wrong. Possible but operationally heavy versus the managed requirement.

questions.stem: A data platform must let analysts query a curated S3 data lake but mask PII columns for everyone outside the privacy team, with permissions managed in one place. Which service provides column-level governance here?

options.explanation: Correct. Lake Formation centralizes lake permissions including column/row-level access controls.
Wrong. Bucket policies act on objects, not columns inside files.
Wrong. IAM cannot mask columns within a dataset.
Wrong. Restructuring data per permission is unmanageable.

questions.stem: A streaming startup's CloudFront bill is high; analysis shows the same large video segments fetched repeatedly from origin because of low cache hit ratio from query-string variations that don't change content. What fix cuts origin egress?

options.text: Configure the cache policy to ignore the irrelevant query strings
options.explanation: Correct. Normalizing cache keys turns duplicate variants into hits, slashing origin fetches.

options.text: Move the origin to a bigger instance
options.explanation: Wrong. Origin capacity does not reduce egress charges.
Wrong. Shorter TTLs reduce hits further.
Wrong. Splitting traffic splits the cache, hurting hit ratio.

questions.stem: An ecommerce checkout calls a third-party payment API that occasionally takes 30+ seconds. The synchronous call ties up web workers and cascades into site-wide slowness. What pattern isolates the dependency?

options.text: Queue payment requests (SQS) and process them with dedicated workers, notifying users asynchronously
options.explanation: Correct. Decoupling caps the blast radius of a slow third party.
Wrong. More workers still block 30 seconds each; the cascade just costs more.
Wrong. Aggressive retries amplify load on a struggling dependency.
Wrong. Payments are non-idempotent unique transactions—caching is unsafe.

questions.stem: A reporting service reads a config file at startup from one EBS volume attached to one instance. The DR plan requires the service to relaunch in another AZ automatically. Why will it fail, and what fixes it?

options.text: EBS is AZ-scoped; move the config to S3 (or EFS) so any AZ can read it
options.explanation: Correct. AZ-locked storage breaks cross-AZ relaunch; regional storage fixes it.
Wrong. The replacement instance in another AZ cannot attach the old volume.

options.text: Use instance store for the config
options.explanation: Wrong. Instance store is even more ephemeral.
Wrong. Restoring snapshots delays recovery and needs orchestration; shared storage is simpler.

questions.stem: Compliance requires that engineers access production only after an approved change ticket, with every command recorded. The team uses SSM Session Manager. What completes the requirement?

options.text: Session logging to S3/CloudWatch plus IAM conditions integrated with the approval workflow
options.explanation: Correct. Logged sessions and gated permissions satisfy record-and-approval controls.

options.text: Trust engineers to paste commands into tickets
options.explanation: Wrong. Self-reporting is not an audit control.
Wrong. Operability requires controlled access, not none.
Wrong. Ad hoc capture is not systematic logging.

questions.stem: A flash-sale platform sees DynamoDB throttling on a hot partition: one celebrity product's counter item takes all the writes. Which design relieves the hot key?

options.text: Shard the counter across N suffixed keys and aggregate on read
options.explanation: Correct. Write sharding spreads a hot item across partitions.

options.text: Switch the table to provisioned mode at higher RCU
options.explanation: Wrong. Per-partition limits still cap a single hot key.
Wrong. Indexes serve queries; they do not absorb writes to one key.
Wrong. PITR is backup, unrelated to throughput.

questions.stem: An ML team needs 8 GPU instances for a 3-week training burst, then nothing for months. Procurement suggests a 1-year reservation 'for the discount.' What should the architect recommend?

options.text: On-Demand (or Capacity Blocks/Spot where tolerable) for the burst; no commitment
options.explanation: Correct. Commitments only pay off for sustained use; a 3-week burst should stay on-demand.
Wrong. Paying 52 weeks for 3 weeks of use is a large net loss.
Wrong. Months of idle hardware is the same mistake in capex form.

options.text: Stretch training over a year to use the reservation
options.explanation: Wrong. Delaying the business to justify a discount inverts priorities.

questions.stem: During a deployment, an operator must update DNS to shift traffic from the blue stack to the green stack, with the ability to send 10% first and roll back instantly. Which Route 53 feature supports this?

options.explanation: Correct. Weights enable gradual cutover and instant rollback by changing values.
Wrong. Failover is for unhealthy-primary switchover, not gradual shifts.
Wrong. Geography is unrelated to canary percentage.
Wrong. Multivalue gives rough random spread without controllable percentages.

questions.stem: A fintech must guarantee its EBS volumes, RDS storage, and S3 objects are encrypted with keys that auditors can see rotation history for. The team currently uses default AWS-managed keys. What change satisfies the auditors?

options.text: Use customer managed KMS keys with rotation enabled across the services
options.explanation: Correct. CMKs expose policy, rotation status, and CloudTrail usage history to auditors.
Wrong. They encrypt fine but offer no customer-controlled policy or attestable rotation control.

options.text: Application-layer encryption with keys in code
options.explanation: Wrong. Keys in code is a finding, not a control.
Wrong. Opposite of the requirement.

questions.stem: A multiplayer game backend needs to push real-time position updates to thousands of connected browsers with two-way messaging. Plain HTTP polling melted the servers. Which managed approach fits?

options.explanation: Correct. Managed WebSockets give bidirectional push without polling load.
Wrong. Faster polling multiplies the load that melted the servers.
Wrong. Real-time per-player state is uncacheable.
Wrong. Browsers cannot securely long-poll SQS per user at scale; not its model.

questions.stem: A company's RTO analysis: a warm standby in a second Region must take production traffic within 5 minutes of a Regional failure, at minimum steady-state cost. Which DR pattern matches?

options.text: Warm standby: scaled-down full stack always running, scaled up + DNS failover on disaster
options.explanation: Correct. Warm standby trades a small steady cost for minutes-level RTO.
Wrong. Restoring from backups takes hours, missing the 5-minute RTO.
Wrong. Meets RTO but at full duplicate cost—violates the cost constraint.

options.text: Pilot light with no app tier running
options.explanation: Wrong. Cheaper but app-tier cold start typically exceeds 5 minutes.

questions.stem: A log pipeline writes 5 TB/day to S3 Standard and almost all reads happen within 48 hours; data must be queryable for 90 days then deleted. What lifecycle minimizes cost?

options.text: Standard for 30 days (minimum), then Standard-IA, expire at 90 days
options.explanation: Correct. Recent-hot/older-cold with automatic expiry matches the access pattern; IA's 30-day minimum is respected.
Wrong. 'Forever' violates the 90-day deletion; and the pattern is known, not unknown.
Wrong. Glacier retrieval costs/latency hurt the day-3-to-90 queries; also minimum-duration charges.

options.text: Keep all in Standard and delete manually
options.explanation: Wrong. Manual deletion fails eventually; Standard for cold days overpays.

questions.stem: A security review of a three-tier VPC app requires: web tier reachable only from the ALB, app tier only from web tier, database only from app tier. What implements this cleanly?

options.text: Security groups referencing other security groups as sources, per tier
options.explanation: Correct. SG-to-SG references express tier-to-tier reachability precisely and survive IP churn.

options.text: One security group for all tiers
options.explanation: Wrong. A shared group cannot express tier isolation.
Wrong. IP-based subnet rules are brittle as instances change.
Wrong. IAM governs API calls, not network reachability.

questions.stem: A legacy app requires a shared POSIX filesystem across 20 Linux EC2 instances with automatic growth and no capacity management. Which storage service fits?

options.explanation: Correct. Elastic NFS shared across instances with no provisioning.
Wrong. Separate volumes are not shared storage.
Wrong. The legacy app requires POSIX file semantics, not object APIs.
Wrong. Ephemeral, per-instance, manual.

questions.stem: A team deploys updates by terminating all instances and launching new ones, causing a 10-minute outage each release. They want zero-downtime releases with automatic rollback on failed health checks. What deployment approach fits on EC2?

options.text: Rolling or blue/green deployments via CodeDeploy with ALB health checks
options.explanation: Correct. Gradual replacement behind a load balancer with health-gated rollback removes the outage.
Wrong. Scheduling an outage is still an outage.

options.text: Bigger instances so fewer to replace
options.explanation: Wrong. Replacement strategy, not size, causes the downtime.
Wrong. That removes the safety the team asked for.

questions.stem: Analysis of a data-transfer bill shows app servers in three AZs all talk constantly to a cache in one AZ, generating large inter-AZ charges. Availability must not regress. What reduces the charges?

options.text: Run cache nodes in each AZ and prefer same-AZ reads (replica per AZ)
options.explanation: Correct. AZ-local reads remove most cross-AZ transfer while keeping multi-AZ resilience.

options.text: Move all app servers into the cache's AZ
options.explanation: Wrong. Cheaper, but collapses availability into one AZ.
Wrong. Helps marginally; topology is the real cost driver.
Wrong. Public-path traffic costs more, not less.

questions.stem: A company must email customers from its app and prove domain ownership with SPF/DKIM, handle bounces, and avoid being blacklisted. Running their own mail server was rejected. Which service is appropriate?

options.text: Amazon SES with verified domain and DKIM enabled
options.explanation: Correct. SES provides authenticated, reputation-managed sending with bounce handling.
Wrong. SNS email is for notifications/subscriptions, not branded transactional mail at scale.
Wrong. That is the self-managed server they rejected, with deliverability risk.
Wrong. WorkMail is mailbox hosting for humans, not app sending.

questions.stem: A SaaS exposes per-tenant APIs; one tenant's runaway script degraded everyone last week. The fix must throttle per API key without app changes. Where is this enforced?

options.text: API Gateway usage plans with per-key rate and burst limits
options.explanation: Correct. Usage plans throttle per tenant key at the managed front door.
Wrong. That is an app change and is enforced too late, inside the service.
Wrong. ALB cannot distinguish tenants by API key for throttling.
Wrong. Capacity absorbs abuse rather than containing it.

questions.stem: An on-prem file server must keep working for local apps while its data becomes available in S3 for cloud analytics, with low-latency local access to hot files. Which hybrid service fits?

options.explanation: Correct. File Gateway exposes SMB/NFS locally with data stored in S3 and a local cache.
Wrong. DataSync moves data; it does not provide an ongoing local file interface.
Wrong. A network link is not a file service.
Wrong. WAN-mounted NFS gives poor local latency and no local cache tier like the gateway.

questions.stem: A regulated workload requires that compute instances never receive public IPs and that engineers still reach them for administration. Which TWO together satisfy this?

options.text: Place instances in private subnets with no public IP assignment
options.explanation: Correct. Private subnets prevent direct internet exposure.

options.text: Use SSM Session Manager (or a bastion via Bastion service) for administration
options.explanation: Correct. Managed access paths reach private instances without public IPs.

options.text: Assign Elastic IPs but block them with NACLs
options.explanation: Wrong. Public addressing with filters violates the no-public-IP requirement.

options.text: Use the EC2 serial console for daily admin
options.explanation: Wrong. Serial console is a break-glass tool, not routine administration.

questions.stem: A startup's databases, caches, and queues are all self-managed on EC2 'to save money,' but two engineers spend most of their time on patching, backups, and failover drills. What should the architect point out?

options.text: Managed services (RDS, ElastiCache, SQS) often cost less in total once engineering time is priced in
options.explanation: Correct. TCO includes the people; undifferentiated heavy lifting is what managed services remove.
Wrong. Instance price ignores the engineering cost dominating this scenario.
Wrong. Scaling toil instead of removing it.

options.text: Move everything to one big instance
options.explanation: Wrong. Consolidation increases blast radius and changes nothing about toil.

questions.stem: An app stores user sessions in instance memory; the ALB uses sticky sessions. During scale-in, users on terminated instances are logged out. What removes the coupling?

options.text: Externalize sessions to ElastiCache (or DynamoDB) and disable stickiness
options.explanation: Correct. Shared session storage makes any instance able to serve any user.
Wrong. Pinning capacity to protect sessions wastes money and still fails on instance crash.
Wrong. Termination still drops the sessions on that instance.
Wrong. Large sensitive state in cookies raises security and size issues; server-side store is standard.

questions.stem: A nightly batch writes directly to the production OLTP database for 3 hours, during which customer-facing latency triples. Both workloads matter. What architectural separation fixes the contention?

options.text: Stage batch output and apply via throttled writes, or load into a separate instance and sync—decouple batch from OLTP peak
options.explanation: Correct. Isolating or smoothing the batch write path protects interactive latency.

options.text: Run the batch faster with more threads
options.explanation: Wrong. Faster batch means harder contention spikes.
Wrong. Communicating degradation is not fixing it.

options.text: Drop database indexes for batch speed
options.explanation: Wrong. That degrades OLTP further.

questions.stem: An organization needs employees to access AWS accounts using their existing Azure AD (Entra ID) identities, with no IAM users created. What is the supported pattern?

options.text: Federate Entra ID with AWS IAM Identity Center via SAML
options.explanation: Correct. Identity Center federates the corporate IdP and maps users to permission sets across accounts.

options.text: Create IAM users matching each employee
options.explanation: Wrong. Duplicated identities are exactly what federation avoids.

options.text: Share a single power-user IAM account
options.explanation: Wrong. Shared identities destroy accountability.

options.text: Use root with MFA for everyone
options.explanation: Wrong. Root is never for daily multi-user access.

questions.stem: A catalog service serves product pages whose data changes a few times daily. Page render queries hit DynamoDB hard, and most requests are for the same few hundred popular products. Which addition cuts read load with minimal code change?

options.text: DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) in front of the table
options.explanation: Correct. DAX is an API-compatible cache, absorbing hot-item reads with microsecond latency.
Wrong. Paying for repeated identical reads instead of caching them.
Wrong. Scans are the most expensive read pattern.
Wrong. Staleness aside, the app would need a rewrite to read exports.

questions.stem: An organization with 60 accounts pays On-Demand rates everywhere; usage is steady. What single billing-level action captures commitment discounts across all accounts with central control?

options.text: Purchase Savings Plans in the management (payer) account—they apply org-wide
options.explanation: Correct. Consolidated billing shares commitment discounts across member accounts.

options.text: Each team buys its own RIs
options.explanation: Wrong. Fragmented purchases risk mismatch and lose central optimization.

options.text: Move all workloads into one account
options.explanation: Wrong. Restructuring isn't needed; billing already consolidates.
Wrong. Not a thing.

questions.stem: A Lambda-based API intermittently fails when a downstream service throttles. Failed events are currently lost. The fix must capture failures for replay without blocking successes. What should be configured?

options.text: A dead-letter queue (or Lambda destinations on failure) for the events
options.explanation: Correct. DLQs capture failed events for inspection and replay.
Wrong. Blocking retries amplify throttling and delay everything.
Wrong. Logged-and-lost is still lost.
Wrong. Memory does not fix downstream throttling.

questions.stem: A company discovers an unknown EC2 instance mining cryptocurrency in their account. Which service would have detected this anomalous behavior automatically?

options.explanation: Correct. GuardDuty's threat detection flags cryptomining and anomalous activity from logs and DNS. `aws route53 change-resource-record-sets --hosted-zone-id Zxxx --change-batch file://rr.json`.
Wrong. Config tracks configuration state, not behavioral threats.
Wrong. Advisor checks best practices, not active threats.
Wrong. The bill shows it eventually—detection should not wait for invoices.

questions.stem: A latency-sensitive HPC simulation needs 100 Gbps-class, microsecond-latency networking between its EC2 nodes. Standard placement gave inconsistent results. What two settings matter most?

options.text: Cluster placement group plus EFA-enabled instances
options.explanation: Correct. Co-located placement and Elastic Fabric Adapter deliver HPC-grade inter-node networking.
Wrong. Spread maximizes separation—the opposite of what HPC interconnect needs.
Wrong. Public routing adds hops and variability.

options.text: Jumbo frames alone on default placement
options.explanation: Wrong. MTU helps but cannot overcome physical distance variability.

questions.stem: A SaaS company runs in us-east-1 only. A risk workshop asks: 'If S3 in our Region has an outage, do customer file downloads still work?' Today the answer is no. With minimal application change, what makes the answer yes?

options.text: Cross-Region Replication to a second Region with failover via Route 53/Multi-Region Access Points
options.explanation: Correct. Replicated objects plus request failover keep downloads alive through a Regional S3 incident. `aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier db --multi-az --apply-immediately`.
Wrong. Versions live in the same Region and share its outage.
Wrong. Storage classes change cost/durability tier, not Regional dependency.
Wrong. URLs to an unavailable Region still fail.

questions.stem: A platform team must cut their largest cost: 200 TB/month of CloudWatch Logs ingestion, mostly debug-level logs nobody reads. Production troubleshooting needs error/warn logs retained 30 days. What approach is right?

options.text: Reduce log level at the source, set retention policies, and route low-value logs to S3 (infrequent access) if needed
options.explanation: Correct. Ingest less, retain shorter, and use cheaper storage for the residue.
Wrong. Ingestion pricing is the dominant cost; compression doesn't change it.
Wrong. Troubleshooting needs error/warn retained.
Wrong. Adds cost atop the same ingestion.

questions.stem: A new policy: production S3 buckets may only be accessed from within the company's VPCs—even valid IAM credentials used from the internet must be denied. How is this enforced?

options.text: Bucket policies with a condition requiring the specific aws:SourceVpce (VPC endpoint)
options.explanation: Correct. Endpoint-conditioned bucket policies deny access from outside the approved network path.
Wrong. Identity policies cannot constrain the network path by themselves here; the resource must enforce it.
Wrong. BPA blocks public grants; credentialed internet access is different.
Wrong. Obscurity is not access control.

questions.stem: A weather API's responses are identical for all users in the same city for 5 minutes. Backend compute is the main cost. What single change cuts backend load most?

options.text: CloudFront (or API Gateway caching) with a 5-minute TTL keyed by city
options.explanation: Correct. Edge/API caching collapses identical requests into one origin hit per city per TTL.
Wrong. Paying more to compute the same answer repeatedly.
Wrong. Query language is orthogonal to repeated identical work.
Wrong. Faster duplication is still duplication.

questions.stem: An events platform must absorb a 100x ingestion spike (ticket scans at stadium gates) without losing a single scan, while the downstream processor handles a steady rate. Scans must be processed at least once. What is the buffer?

options.text: SQS standard queue between scanners and processors
options.explanation: Correct. Elastic buffering with at-least-once delivery absorbs the spike and feeds steady consumers.
Wrong. The database becomes the choke point at 100x.

options.text: In-memory buffer in the API tier
options.explanation: Wrong. Memory buffers lose scans on crash or scale-in.
Wrong. The requirement is zero loss.

questions.stem: After moving a steady web workload from x86 to Graviton instances, the team validated identical performance. What is the primary financial outcome they should expect?

options.text: Roughly 20-40% better price-performance for the same workload
options.explanation: Correct. Graviton's main proposition is lower cost per unit of performance.
Wrong. Transfer pricing is unchanged by CPU architecture.
Wrong. Commitments remain a separate purchase.
Wrong. Graviton instances are priced lower per performance.

questions.stem: A mobile app needs to let users upload images directly to S3 without routing files through the backend and without embedding AWS credentials in the app. What is the standard pattern?

options.text: Backend issues short-lived pre-signed PUT URLs
options.explanation: Correct. Pre-signed URLs grant scoped, expiring upload rights with no credentials in the client.

options.text: Embed an access key in the app
options.explanation: Wrong. Shipped credentials are public credentials.
Wrong. Public write invites abuse and junk.
Wrong. The scenario explicitly avoids backend file routing.

questions.stem: A company needs file transfers from hundreds of partners who only support SFTP, landing in S3 for processing. Running SFTP servers on EC2 was rejected as toil. Which service fits?

options.explanation: Correct. Managed SFTP endpoints deliver directly to S3 with no servers.
Wrong. S3 has no native SFTP endpoint; Transfer Family provides it.

options.text: Partners switch to the S3 API
options.explanation: Wrong. The constraint says partners only support SFTP.
Wrong. That is the self-managed toil they rejected.

questions.stem: An ad-serving engine must decide in under 20 ms using a 5 GB model that changes hourly. Loading from S3 per request is far too slow. Instances are in an ASG. What is the right serving pattern?

options.text: Load the model into instance memory on boot/refresh; serve from RAM
options.explanation: Correct. In-memory serving meets the latency; hourly refresh handles updates.

options.text: Read the model from S3 per request
options.explanation: Wrong. Network reads per request blow the 20 ms budget.
Wrong. A relational database is not a model-serving cache.
Wrong. FUSE reads remain network-bound per access.

questions.stem: A report shows the company pays for 3 idle NAT gateways in dev VPCs whose instances only need to reach S3, DynamoDB, and internal services. What removes the NAT cost without losing required access?

options.text: Replace with gateway endpoints (S3, DynamoDB) and interface endpoints/peering for internal services; delete the NATs
options.explanation: Correct. Private endpoints cover the actual destinations; NAT was unnecessary.

options.text: One shared NAT for all dev VPCs
options.explanation: Wrong. Cheaper, but endpoints make NAT unnecessary entirely for these destinations.
Wrong. Swapping managed for self-managed toil to keep an unneeded component.
Wrong. Exposing dev instances to cut cost is a security regression.

questions.stem: Security requires that a compromised web-tier instance must not be able to read the customer-PII DynamoDB table—only the app tier may. All tiers currently share one instance role. What is the fix?

options.text: Separate IAM roles per tier; only the app-tier role gets table access, scoped to needed actions
options.explanation: Correct. Role separation enforces least privilege at the tier boundary.
Wrong. The shared role still decrypts via normal API access.
Wrong. Obscurity, not authorization.
Wrong. Endpoints control the path; the shared role still authorizes access.

questions.stem: A monolith on one large EC2 instance reboots for patches monthly, causing 15-minute outages. Splitting the monolith is out of scope this year. What reduces patch downtime to near zero without re-architecture?

options.text: Run two instances behind an ALB and patch one at a time
options.explanation: Correct. N+1 behind a load balancer turns reboots into traffic shifts.
Wrong. A faster reboot is still an outage.
Wrong. Trading availability risk for security risk.
Wrong. Size does not change single-instance reboot downtime.

questions.stem: A bioinformatics workload reads the same 30 TB reference dataset from S3 across thousands of containers per run. Repeated S3 GETs are slow and costly. Runs happen weekly. What architecture improves throughput?

options.text: Stage the dataset on FSx for Lustre (S3-linked) per run; containers read from the filesystem
options.explanation: Correct. A parallel filesystem turns repeated object reads into high-throughput local-style access.
Wrong. The pattern, not the limit, is the bottleneck/cost.

options.text: Copy 30 TB into each container image
options.explanation: Wrong. Image bloat at that scale is infeasible.
Wrong. CDN is for internet distribution, not intra-VPC HPC reads.

questions.stem: A team asks whether to keep paying for a m5.xlarge RDS instance for a tiny internal app with single-digit daily users. Downtime of a few seconds on first use is acceptable. What is the cost-appropriate move?

options.text: Aurora Serverless v2 (or smallest burstable class) sized to actual load
options.explanation: Correct. Scale-to-tiny capacity matches negligible usage; brief resume latency is acceptable per the scenario.
Wrong. Paying for capacity insurance the workload never claims.

options.text: Move the app to the production database
options.explanation: Wrong. Coupling a toy app to prod risks the important system.
Wrong. Discounting oversize is still oversize.

questions.stem: An incident review found nobody noticed a public-read ACL added to a sensitive bucket for 3 weeks. Which service continuously evaluates such configuration drift and can auto-remediate it?

options.text: AWS Config rules with remediation actions
options.explanation: Correct. Config detects non-compliant configuration changes and can trigger automatic remediation.
Wrong. The API call was logged—nobody was evaluating it; logging is not evaluation.
Wrong. Three weeks of exposure is the cost of periodic-only review.
Wrong. Public ACLs do not necessarily change spend.

questions.stem: A global app uses DynamoDB global tables in three Regions with active-active writes. Product asks: 'What consistency behavior must the app tolerate by design?'

options.text: Last-writer-wins conflict resolution and eventual cross-Region consistency
options.explanation: Correct. Global tables replicate asynchronously; concurrent Regional writes resolve by timestamp.

options.text: Strong global consistency on every read
options.explanation: Wrong. Strong consistency is per-Region; cross-Region replication is eventual.
Wrong. Global tables do not do multi-Region synchronous transactions.
Wrong. Reads are always served; staleness, not blocking, is the trade-off.
